The key distinction lies in the primary habitats of these bacteria; Streptococcus sanguinis primarily resides in the mouth and throat, which makes it an outlier as far as regular colonization of the colon goes. In contrast, Porphyromonas species such as P. intermedia and P. gingivalis, along with Fusobacterium, have been observed more frequently within the gastrointestinal tract due to their ability to survive outside the oral environment through mechanisms including adherence to mucus layers and host immune evasion strategies.
